content.text: Google Cloud Platform (GCP) is Google's suite of managed cloud services spanning compute, storage, databases, messaging, AI, and security. For teams building agentic AI systems, GCP provides a tightly integrated stack — Cloud Run for serverless containers, GKE for Kubernetes workloads, Vertex AI for Gemini and embeddings, Cloud SQL and Memorystore for state, and BigQuery for analytics — all connected through a global private backbone and governed by IAM.
GCP is a public cloud from Google, organized around the concept of

. A project is the fundamental billing and access boundary: every resource (a Cloud Run service, a Cloud SQL instance, a bucket) lives inside exactly one project. Projects can be grouped under folders and folders under an organization, giving enterprise teams a hierarchy that mirrors business units or environments (dev / staging / prod).
Within a project, services are deployed to specific
(individual datacenters inside a region). A single
(Virtual Private Cloud) network can span all regions, letting you connect services privately without traversing the public internet.
The major service families relevant to agentic workloads are:
— Compute Engine (VMs), Cloud Run (serverless containers), GKE (managed Kubernetes)
— Cloud SQL, AlloyDB, Spanner (relational); Firestore, Bigtable (NoSQL); BigQuery (analytics)
— Vertex AI (Gemini, embeddings, Vector Search, model endpoints, Pipelines)
— IAM, Secret Manager, Cloud KMS, Identity-Aware Proxy, Cloud Armor
— Cloud Logging, Cloud Monitoring, Cloud Trace, Error Reporting

Building an agentic system that runs reliably at scale requires solving a cluster of infrastructure problems simultaneously: stateless serving with elastic scale, durable task queues for long-running tool calls, a fast KV cache for session memory, a relational store for audit trails, cheap object storage for RAG corpora, and secure credential management. Wiring these together from bare-metal servers or even raw VMs is weeks of operations work before a single agent line is written.

GCP collapses that plumbing into managed services with per-second billing, automatic patches, and built-in SLAs. Cloud Run, for instance, accepts a container image and handles routing, TLS, horizontal scaling, health checks, and graceful rollouts — operations tasks that would otherwise require a full platform team. Teams already anchored to Google Workspace or BigQuery data warehouses gain additional value by keeping agents and data in the same network, eliminating egress costs and simplifying compliance.

Vertex AI is the clearest differentiator: it exposes Google's own Gemini family (including multimodal and long-context variants) through a managed API that can be called privately over VPC, satisfying data-residency requirements that rule out public API calls for regulated industries.
— Cloud Run bills per 100ms of request processing; idle services cost nothing, making it ideal for bursty agent APIs.
— Gemini, text-embedding-004, and Vector Search are first-party, callable over private VPC without public internet exposure.
— Google's backbone carries inter-region traffic without touching the public internet; latency between us-central1 and europe-west1 stays under 80 ms.
— GKE pods and Cloud Run services authenticate to other GCP APIs via short-lived OIDC tokens; no long-lived service-account JSON keys needed.
— structured agent logs streamed to BigQuery enable SQL queries over millions of tool invocations, success/failure rates, and latency distributions.
— private Docker registry with vulnerability scanning; images never leave the project boundary.
— Cloud SQL, Memorystore, and Pub/Sub require no capacity planning for typical agent load; they scale independently.
— the maximum request timeout is 3600 seconds (1 hour), and SSE streams count against this. Very long multi-turn conversations need to be chunked or moved to async via Pub/Sub.

— a Cloud Run revision with min-instances=0 takes 1–4 seconds to start a Python container. Latency-sensitive chat agents need at least one warm instance, which negates the true scale-to-zero cost benefit.
— AWS Marketplace has more pre-built SaaS integrations than GCP Marketplace. Teams relying on niche managed services may find AWS has a ready-made option that GCP lacks.
Egress cost for multi-cloud LLM calls
— if agents call non-GCP LLM providers (OpenAI, Anthropic), each response travels over the public internet and incurs GCP egress fees (≈$0.08–0.12/GB outbound).
— a GKE Autopilot cluster has a flat control-plane fee (~$74/month); Autopilot nodes are billed per pod resource request. For small teams, this is more expensive than a single Cloud Run service.
— Pub/Sub message format, Vertex AI client libraries, and Cloud Run IAM annotations are GCP-specific. Migrating a mature agent stack to AWS or Azure requires rewriting integration code.
An external HTTP request enters GCP through the global

(HTTPS, anycast IP). Cloud Armor applies WAF rules and rate limits. The load balancer routes to the nearest healthy Cloud Run revision. The Cloud Run runtime pulls the container from Artifact Registry, starts it in a sandboxed instance, and forwards the request. The container process reads secrets from Secret Manager at startup and calls downstream services over the
via Private Service Connect, so no data crosses the public internet.
When an agent tool call is expected to run longer than a few seconds (web search, code execution, document indexing), the API handler publishes a message to
. A GKE Deployment or Cloud Run Job subscribes to the topic and processes the task asynchronously. Results are written back to
(binary artifacts). The original HTTP client polls a status endpoint or uses a WebSocket / SSE stream to receive the result.
Three layers of state are common in agentic systems on GCP:
— Memorystore Redis holds the active conversation context window (last N messages) keyed by session ID. Redis GET/SET latency is typically under 1 ms from the same region.
— Cloud SQL PostgreSQL stores session metadata, tool call history, and agent run audit records. Reads and writes are on the order of 2–10 ms over the VPC.

— Cloud Storage holds PDF/HTML documents for RAG, serialized tool outputs, and observability traces. Cloud Storage access is eventually consistent for object listings but strongly consistent for individual object reads after writes.
Every Cloud Run service and GKE workload runs as a dedicated

(SA). IAM bindings grant each SA only the roles it needs — e.g. the agent API SA gets roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor and roles/cloudsql.client, but not storage admin. Workload Identity Federation binds the Kubernetes ServiceAccount to the GCP SA so pods obtain short-lived tokens automatically; no JSON key file is needed inside the container.

The diagram below shows a full agentic AI stack on GCP: a Cloud Run API fronted by Cloud Armor, asynchronous workers on GKE consuming Pub/Sub, data services in the red tier, Vertex AI for models, and the security/observability layer at the bottom.

attrs.alt: GCP Agentic AI Architecture: Cloud Run API, GKE workers, Pub/Sub, Cloud SQL, Memorystore, Cloud Storage, BigQuery, Vertex AI, and security services

content.text: Traffic flows left to right: the browser hits the load balancer, which routes to Cloud Run. Long-running tool calls are offloaded to Pub/Sub and processed by GKE workers. All compute reads from the data tier over private VPC; Vertex AI handles LLM and embedding calls; Secret Manager, IAM, VPC, and Cloud Logging underpin the entire stack.
Google Workspace is the primary productivity suite
— Workspace APIs (Gmail, Drive, Calendar, Docs) are first-party on GCP and callable without complex cross-cloud auth.
— agents that query or write to BigQuery are most efficiently co-located in GCP, avoiding egress and simplifying IAM.
— Vertex AI gives private access to Gemini 1.5 / 2.0 Flash and Pro, with data-residency guarantees unavailable through the public API.
— small teams that cannot staff a platform engineer can run a production agent API on Cloud Run with a single
— VPC Service Controls, CMEK encryption, and Vertex AI's private endpoints satisfy SOC 2, HIPAA BAA, and GDPR localization.
The team is already deep in AWS and has EKS, RDS, SQS, and Bedrock configured; switching incurs retraining and migration cost.
The workload requires GPU instances not available in the target region; AWS and Azure often have newer GPU SKUs earlier in more regions.
Third-party SaaS integrations are available only via AWS Marketplace.
Create a dedicated GCP project for the agent service and enable the required APIs. Separating dev and prod into distinct projects provides hard billing and IAM isolation.

# Create project (requires billing account)
gcloud projects create my-agent-prod --name="Agent Production"
gcloud config set project my-agent-prod

# Enable required service APIs
gcloud services enable \
  run.googleapis.com \
  sqladmin.googleapis.com \
  redis.googleapis.com \
  secretmanager.googleapis.com \
  pubsub.googleapis.com \
  aiplatform.googleapis.com \
  artifactregistry.googleapis.com \
  container.googleapis.com \
  logging.googleapis.com \
  monitoring.googleapis.com

# Dedicated SA for the Cloud Run agent API
gcloud iam service-accounts create agent-api-sa \
  --display-name="Agent API Service Account"

SA="agent-api-sa@my-agent-prod.iam.gserviceaccount.com"

# Grant minimal roles
gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding my-agent-prod \
  --member="serviceAccount:$SA" \
  --role="roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor"

gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding my-agent-prod \
  --member="serviceAccount:$SA" \
  --role="roles/cloudsql.client"

gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding my-agent-prod \
  --member="serviceAccount:$SA" \
  --role="roles/aiplatform.user"

gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding my-agent-prod \
  --member="serviceAccount:$SA" \
  --role="roles/pubsub.publisher"

# Store API keys; reference by name in Cloud Run, never hard-code
echo -n "sk-ant-..." | gcloud secrets create anthropic-api-key --data-file=-
echo -n "postgres-password-here" | gcloud secrets create db-password --data-file=-
Step 4 — build and deploy to Cloud Run

# Build image and push to Artifact Registry
gcloud artifacts repositories create agent-images \
  --repository-format=docker --location=us-central1

gcloud builds submit --tag us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/my-agent-prod/agent-images/agent-api:v1

# Deploy with secrets injected as environment variables
gcloud run deploy agent-api \
  --image us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/my-agent-prod/agent-images/agent-api:v1 \
  --region us-central1 \
  --service-account agent-api-sa@my-agent-prod.iam.gserviceaccount.com \
  --set-secrets ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=anthropic-api-key:latest,DB_PASSWORD=db-password:latest \
  --set-env-vars DB_HOST=/cloudsql/my-agent-prod:us-central1:agent-db \
  --add-cloudsql-instances my-agent-prod:us-central1:agent-db \
  --min-instances 1 \
  --max-instances 20 \
  --timeout 3600 \
  --no-allow-unauthenticated
keeps one warm container to avoid cold-start latency on the first message;
allows SSE streams up to one hour;
means callers must present a valid GCP identity token, enabling IAP-based auth.
Example 1 — calling Vertex AI Gemini from Python
This minimal example authenticates via Application Default Credentials (ADC), which automatically uses the Workload Identity token when running inside Cloud Run or GKE. No key file is needed.

# pip install google-cloud-aiplatform
import vertexai
from vertexai.generative_models import GenerativeModel

# Project and location come from ADC / env vars in production.
vertexai.init(project="my-agent-prod", location="us-central1")

model = GenerativeModel("gemini-2.0-flash")

response = model.generate_content(
    "Summarize the benefits of serverless containers for agentic AI workloads.",
    generation_config={
        "max_output_tokens": 512,
        "temperature": 0.2,
    },
)
print(response.text)
Example 2 — publishing to Pub/Sub and processing asynchronously

This two-part example shows the producer (Cloud Run handler) and the consumer (GKE worker). The Pub/Sub message carries a JSON payload; the worker acknowledges only after the task completes, so unprocessed messages are retried automatically.

# === PRODUCER: Cloud Run handler (FastAPI) ===
# pip install google-cloud-pubsub fastapi uvicorn
import json, uuid
from fastapi import FastAPI
from google.cloud import pubsub_v1

app = FastAPI()
publisher = pubsub_v1.PublisherClient()
TOPIC = "projects/my-agent-prod/topics/agent-jobs"

@app.post("/run")
async def enqueue_run(payload: dict):
    run_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
    message = json.dumps({"run_id": run_id, **payload}).encode()
    future = publisher.publish(TOPIC, message, run_id=run_id)
    future.result()  # block until published
    return {"run_id": run_id, "status": "queued"}


# === CONSUMER: GKE worker (runs as a Deployment) ===
from google.cloud import pubsub_v1
import json, time

subscriber = pubsub_v1.SubscriberClient()
SUB = "projects/my-agent-prod/subscriptions/agent-jobs-sub"

def process(message: pubsub_v1.subscriber.message.Message):
    data = json.loads(message.data.decode())
    run_id = data["run_id"]
    print(f"Processing run {run_id}")
    try:
        # --- agent logic here ---
        time.sleep(1)  # placeholder
        message.ack()
    except Exception as exc:
        print(f"Run {run_id} failed: {exc}")
        message.nack()  # triggers redelivery

streaming_pull = subscriber.subscribe(SUB, callback=process)
print("Worker listening...")
streaming_pull.result()  # block forever

Consider an internal document-QA agent deployed at a 500-person company. Users ask questions about internal policies; the agent searches a RAG corpus of ~50,000 PDF chunks stored in Vertex AI Vector Search and answers with citations. Architecture decisions:

hosts the FastAPI agent API. Traffic is bursty (peaks during business hours, near-zero nights and weekends). min-instances=1 keeps latency under 200 ms for the first request after idle. max-instances=10 caps compute spend.

provides Gemini 2.0 Flash for generation and text-embedding-004 for embedding. Both are called over VPC, satisfying the legal team's requirement that document content never traverse the public internet.
serves the embedding index. The index is rebuilt nightly via a Cloud Run Job that re-embeds updated PDFs from the Cloud Storage corpus bucket.

stores conversation sessions, user-query history, and chunk-citation records for audit. The Cloud SQL Auth Proxy sidecar (injected automatically by gcloud run deploy --add-cloudsql-instances) handles TLS and IAM auth.
holds the Cloud SQL password and any external API keys. The Cloud Run SA has secretAccessor on those secrets only.
receives structured JSON logs from the agent, including
, latency, and token counts. A log-based metric feeds a Cloud Monitoring alert if p99 latency exceeds 8 seconds.

This setup handles 50 concurrent users with a monthly infra bill under $400 (Cloud Run requests, Vector Search QPS, Cloud SQL db-g1-small instance, and Cloud Storage). A team of two engineers maintains it without a dedicated platform engineer.
Using the default compute service account
Every project has a default Compute Engine service account (

) that is automatically granted Editor on the project. Deploying Cloud Run or GKE with this SA means any container compromise gets broad project access. Always create a dedicated SA with only the roles the service needs.
Leaving Cloud Run timeout at the default
The default Cloud Run request timeout is 300 seconds. An SSE-streaming agent that takes more than 5 minutes to finish a complex tool chain silently closes the connection. Set
(max) and configure the calling client to reconnect if needed.
Storing secrets in environment variables set at deploy time
output and in Cloud Console. Always use
to bind a Secret Manager secret by name; the value is injected at runtime and never stored in the service config.
Creating a public Cloud Storage bucket for RAG documents
New buckets are private by default, but developers sometimes add
while debugging. Documents intended for internal RAG should have Uniform Bucket-Level Access enabled and no public IAM binding. Use signed URLs for time-limited external sharing.
No concurrency limit on Cloud Run
Cloud Run's default concurrency per instance is 80 requests. A Python (GIL-bound) FastAPI service may saturate CPU long before reaching 80 concurrent in-flight agent tasks. Set
to match the realistic per-container parallelism (often 4–16 for CPU-heavy agent code) to prevent latency spikes.
— dev and prod should be separate GCP projects. IAM mistakes in dev cannot affect prod resources, and billing is naturally split.
— bind the Kubernetes ServiceAccount to a GCP service account with Workload Identity Federation. This eliminates JSON key rotation entirely.
Cloud Armor on the load balancer
— add a security policy with OWASP CRS rules and a rate-limit rule (e.g. 100 req/min per IP) to protect public agent APIs from abuse.

— emit logs as JSON objects (include run_id, tool_name, latency_ms, token_count). Cloud Logging parses JSON automatically and makes fields queryable; log-based metrics and BigQuery export become trivial.
— configure a log sink from Cloud Logging to BigQuery for long-term retention and SQL-based analytics over agent behavior.
— deploy Cloud Run with the full sha256 digest (
) so rollbacks are deterministic and supply-chain attacks via tag mutation are prevented.
Use Artifact Registry, not Container Registry
— Container Registry (gcr.io) is deprecated. Artifact Registry supports multiple artifact formats, fine-grained IAM, and vulnerability scanning.
Set resource limits on GKE pods
on GKE workloads. Missing limits mean a runaway agent worker can starve other pods on the same node.

Cloud Run scales the number of container instances proportionally to the number of concurrent requests divided by the configured concurrency per instance. If concurrency=8 and 40 requests arrive simultaneously, Cloud Run targets 5 instances. Scaling decisions are made in under 1 second, and new instances reach ready state in 1–4 seconds for a typical Python container. The scale-down delay is configurable (default: 60 seconds of idle before an instance is removed).

Vertex AI Gemini endpoints are quota-controlled per region. The default quota for Gemini 2.0 Flash is 360 requests per minute. For higher-throughput agents, request quota increases through Cloud Console or consider distributing load across multiple regions. Implement exponential backoff with jitter on 429 responses from Vertex AI.

Cloud SQL accepts a limited number of connections per instance tier. A db-g1-small instance supports ~25 connections; db-n1-standard-2 supports ~400. With Cloud Run potentially spinning up dozens of instances each opening its own connection pool, exhausting the connection limit is a common scaling failure. Use
in combination with a connection pool library (PgBouncer or SQLAlchemy pool_size=2, max_overflow=3) to stay within limits.

# Safe SQLAlchemy pool config for Cloud Run (many instances, few connections each)
from sqlalchemy import create_engine

engine = create_engine(
    "postgresql+pg8000://user:pass@/dbname?unix_sock=/cloudsql/proj:region:instance/.s.PGSQL.5432",
    pool_size=2,        # connections per Cloud Run instance
    max_overflow=3,     # burst allowance per instance
    pool_timeout=10,    # fail fast if no connection available
    pool_pre_ping=True, # detect stale connections
)

A single Pub/Sub topic scales to millions of messages per second automatically. The bottleneck for agent workloads is typically the consumer side: the number of GKE pods and their per-pod processing rate. Scale GKE workers with a Horizontal Pod Autoscaler driven by Pub/Sub subscription message backlog, exposed as a custom metric via the Stackdriver adapter.
— the primary command-line interface for all GCP resources; install via Google Cloud SDK. Essential for scripting deployments and debugging.
— the official Terraform provider for GCP (
) covers Cloud Run, GKE, Cloud SQL, Pub/Sub, and nearly every other service. Preferred over gcloud for repeatable infrastructure-as-code.
) follow a consistent pattern: instantiate a client, call methods, receive protocol-buffer responses.
— the high-level SDK for Vertex AI; handles auth, retries, and streaming. Preferred over raw REST calls.
— GCP-native CI/CD that runs build steps in containers; integrates with Artifact Registry for image publishing and Cloud Run for deployment.
— a Kubernetes operator that manages GCP resources (SQL instances, buckets, topics) as Kubernetes CRDs, enabling GitOps workflows for infrastructure alongside application code.
— a console UI and CLI for connecting Cloud Run services to Cloud SQL, Memorystore, and other services with auto-generated Secret Manager wiring.
The table below compares the most common choices for agentic AI deployments across the three major cloud providers and a self-hosted Kubernetes option.
Google Workspace shops, Gemini users, data-residency
Broadest service catalog, mature ML ops

Every Cloud Run service and GKE workload should run as a dedicated service account with bindings scoped to the minimum required roles. Audit IAM bindings quarterly using IAM Recommender, which identifies unused permissions and suggests removal. Enable Organization Policy constraints to prevent project-level public access and service account key creation.

For regulated workloads, wrap sensitive projects in a VPC Service Controls perimeter. This prevents data exfiltration even if a service account token is leaked — API calls from outside the perimeter to protected services (BigQuery, Cloud Storage, Vertex AI) are blocked regardless of IAM permissions.

Secret Manager supports automatic rotation via Cloud Functions triggers. Configure rotation schedules for database passwords and third-party API keys. Cloud Run services reference secrets by version alias (latest); after rotation, a single new revision deployment picks up the new value without downtime.

A single Cloud Run service in one region is a single point of failure for that region. For SLA targets above 99.9%, deploy to two regions behind a Global HTTPS Load Balancer. Cloud SQL offers cross-region read replicas; promoting a replica to primary takes approximately 1–2 minutes. Cloud Storage is globally replicated when using multi-region or dual-region buckets (

Attach a Cloud Armor security policy to the external load balancer. At minimum, enable the pre-configured OWASP ModSecurity Core Rule Set (CRS) and add a rate-limit rule per source IP. For agent APIs that are only called by known services (not browsers), consider an allow-list policy that blocks all traffic except from known CIDR ranges.

Enable Data Access audit logs (DATA_READ, DATA_WRITE) on Cloud Storage and BigQuery for compliance. Admin Activity logs (CREATE, DELETE, UPDATE on resources) are always on and cannot be disabled. Route audit logs to a locked, write-once Cloud Storage bucket in a separate security project to prevent tampering.
Cloud Run is the default compute choice
for agentic APIs on GCP: serverless, scale-to-zero, supports SSE streams, and deploys in seconds with a single gcloud command.
Use dedicated service accounts per service
with IAM roles scoped to exactly what each service needs. Never use the default compute SA or store JSON keys inside containers.
is the correct pattern for injecting credentials into Cloud Run; never use --set-env-vars for sensitive values.
Vertex AI is the differentiator for Google-centric teams
: Gemini, text-embedding-004, and Vector Search are callable over VPC with data-residency guarantees unavailable through the public API.
Offload long-running tool calls to Pub/Sub + GKE workers
to stay within Cloud Run's request timeout and to enable independent scaling of the API and processing tiers.
(pool_size=2–4 per instance) to avoid exhausting database connections when Cloud Run scales to many instances.
Export structured JSON logs to BigQuery
for SQL-queryable analytics over agent tool latency, token usage, and error rates.
, deploy Cloud Run to two regions behind the Global HTTPS Load Balancer and use multi-region Cloud Storage.
